summary: At the Château du Glandier, Professor Stangerson and his daughter Mathilde were completing scientific work on the dissociation of matter. Mathilde, secretly already married in America to the criminal Ballmeyer under the name Jean Roussel, was engaged to Robert Darzac after believing Ballmeyer dead. Ballmeyer reappeared in France disguised as the famous detective Frédéric Larsan and blackmailed Mathilde with her past, threatening her father’s papers and her marriage. On October 25, 1892, he entered the pavilion during the late-afternoon absence of the Stangersons, using the vestibule window and disguising evidence with Daddy Jacques’s old boots, cap, and handkerchief. When Mathilde returned alone before dinner, he attacked her in the Yellow Room; she fired Daddy Jacques’s revolver and wounded his hand. He fled through the laboratory and vestibule window, leaving blood, footprints, the mutton-bone weapon, and misleading traces. Mathilde concealed the attack to protect her secret, worked with her father until midnight, then locked herself into the Yellow Room. During a nightmare she cried out, overturned furniture, accidentally discharged the revolver into the ceiling, fainted, and injured her temple on the marble table. This created the illusion of an impossible midnight attack in a locked room. Later, Ballmeyer/Larsan continued pursuing Mathilde, staged evidence against Darzac, retrieved and returned stolen scientific papers, drugged Rouletabille, and stabbed Mathilde in her château room. During the same night he killed the forest-keeper, who encountered him in the court, and escaped by climbing from the court using a prepared spike and returning as Larsan through his own window. Darzac was arrested because Larsan arranged compromising absences and planted circumstantial evidence. At trial, Rouletabille exposed Larsan as the murderer, but delayed naming him until Larsan had time to flee. Rouletabille then explained the two-phase Yellow Room mystery, the later attack, the keeper’s murder, the false evidence, and the motive. Darzac was acquitted; Larsan/Ballmeyer escaped; the hidden history of Mathilde’s marriage to Jean Roussel/Ballmeyer explained her silence and Darzac’s refusal to defend himself fully.

witnesses.name: Joseph Rouletabille
witnesses.statement: Reporter-investigator who reconstructs the case: the Yellow Room attack occurred earlier than the apparent midnight attack; the midnight events were Mathilde’s nightmare and accidental revolver discharge; the temple wound came from falling against the marble table; the real assailant was Frédéric Larsan, actually Ballmeyer/Jean Roussel; Larsan staged evidence against Daddy Jacques and Robert Darzac, drugged Rouletabille, stabbed Mathilde later, killed the keeper, escaped by a prepared climbing route, and fled once warned. He later researches Mathilde’s American past and identifies the full motive.

witnesses.name: Sainclair
witnesses.statement: Narrator-observer and Rouletabille’s friend. He reports the investigation, accompanies Rouletabille to the Glandier, observes the physical scene, the suspects, the later château events, the trial, and Rouletabille’s explanations. He also verifies the cane purchase through Monsieur Cassette and preserves Rouletabille’s account.

witnesses.name: Jacques-Louis Moustier, called Daddy Jacques
witnesses.statement: Servant in the pavilion. He says Mathilde entered the Yellow Room at midnight, locked and bolted the door, and soon afterward cried for help while he and Professor Stangerson were in the laboratory. He reports the broken door, the locked window and blinds, the overturned furniture, blood, footprints, handkerchief, cap, and his stolen revolver. Later he conceals that he helped Madame Mathieu leave the grounds after the keeper’s murder.

witnesses.name: Professor Stangerson
witnesses.statement: Victim’s father and eyewitness to the apparent midnight incident. He confirms working with Mathilde until midnight, Daddy Jacques’s presence in the laboratory, the locked Yellow Room door, the breaking-in, the discovery of Mathilde, the two bullets, the keys to the pavilion, the afternoon timeline, and the conversation with the keeper that briefly separated him from Mathilde before the real attack.

witnesses.name: Mademoiselle Mathilde Stangerson
witnesses.statement: Victim. She admits fearing someone, borrowing Daddy Jacques’s revolver, seeing suspicious shadows, and being attacked by a man. However, she falsely or misleadingly places the attack at midnight and conceals the earlier attack, the identity and motive of Jean Roussel/Ballmeyer, and her secret marriage, in order to protect her father and Darzac.

witnesses.name: Monsieur Robert Darzac
witnesses.statement: Mathilde’s fiancé. He knows Mathilde’s secret and receives or understands the significance of the phrase about the presbytery and garden. He helps Rouletabille gain access to the Glandier but refuses to explain his absences or reveal Mathilde’s secret even when accused. His silence protects Mathilde but makes him appear guilty.

witnesses.name: Frédéric Larsan, actually Ballmeyer, also known as Jean Roussel
witnesses.statement: Culprit posing as a celebrated detective. He supplies investigative theories, finds or manipulates evidence, argues against accomplices when useful, frames Darzac, and offers a false solution implicating Darzac. His actions also provide evidence: his cane, eyeglasses, planted traces, false sleep, drugging of Rouletabille, prepared escape spike, flight from court, and hidden identity reveal him as the assailant, blackmailer, and killer.

witnesses.name: Monsieur de Marquet
witnesses.statement: Examining magistrate. He provides official early facts: the sealed Yellow Room, the intact windows and blinds, the pavilion’s doors and windows, the vestibule-window blood traces, the bullets, the wounds, and the initial investigative theory that the murderer hid under the bed and escaped impossibly. He wrongly arrests the concierges and later Darzac on circumstantial evidence.

witnesses.name: Monsieur Maleine
witnesses.statement: Registrar to Monsieur de Marquet. He says he examined or sounded the walls, ceiling, and floor and found no secret passage, trap, or structural escape route. His information supports the locked-room impossibility.

witnesses.name: The family doctor
witnesses.statement: Medical source who reports Mathilde’s condition, allows her questioning when she improves, and states that the throat pressure nearly strangled her and that the temple wound was serious but not necessarily caused by the revolver. His medical observations help separate the actual injuries from false assumptions about their timing.

witnesses.name: Monsieur Bernier
witnesses.statement: Concierge. He helps break into the Yellow Room and later admits he and his wife were poaching near the pavilion, explaining their suspicious presence. During the later château watch he is stationed by Rouletabille, hears shots, fires at a fleeing figure, and testifies that he could not have missed a second body in the narrow court, intensifying the mystery of the keeper’s murder and Larsan’s escape.

witnesses.name: Madame Bernier
witnesses.statement: Concierge’s wife. She accompanies Daddy Jacques to inspect the Yellow Room window and confirms it could not have been used. Later, at Rouletabille’s request, she searches Mathilde’s room and finds the eyeglasses that point toward Larsan/Ballmeyer.

witnesses.name: Daddy Mathieu
witnesses.statement: Landlord of the Donjon Inn and husband of Madame Mathieu. He gives local information about the keeper, reveals hostility toward him, confirms the Berniers’ poaching indirectly through the inn’s rabbit supply, and at first accuses the keeper of being the murderer. His jealousy and threats later make him a false suspect in the keeper’s death.

witnesses.name: Madame Mathieu
witnesses.statement: Landlady of the Donjon Inn. She eventually confesses that she was the keeper’s mistress, that she came secretly to the château disguised in a black shawl, used cat-like cries as a signal, saw the fleeing shadow after the keeper’s murder, saw the keeper’s body, and was helped away by Daddy Jacques. Her concealed affair first obscures the case but later explains the phantom and some footprints.

witnesses.name: Mother Angenoux
witnesses.statement: Local old woman associated with the cat called the Bête du Bon Dieu. She says she was ill, that her cat stayed with her, and that she heard an identical cat cry outside on the night of the crime while her own cat was on her knees. This shows that the cry was imitated by a human signal rather than made by the cat.

witnesses.name: Arthur William Rance
witnesses.statement: American acquaintance and former suitor of Mathilde. He appears at the Glandier, gives information about his movements, says he last saw the keeper around eleven when arranging for his valise to be taken to Saint-Michel station, and participates in the court scene after the later attack. Rouletabille’s American inquiries later show Rance had loved Mathilde and taken to drink, but he is not the culprit.

witnesses.name: Monsieur Cassette
witnesses.statement: Paris cane dealer. He tells Sainclair that a man matching Robert Darzac’s appearance bought a yellow bamboo cane like Larsan’s on the evening of the crime. The statement is accurate as to the sale and resemblance, but the apparent implication against Darzac is misleading because the buyer was Larsan/Ballmeyer in disguise or in a Darzac-like appearance.

witnesses.name: Paris Post Office employee
witnesses.statement: Provides information that a man resembling Darzac came to ask about the poste restante letter after Mathilde had already collected it. The fact is material because it supports Rouletabille’s double theory: someone resembling Darzac, later understood to be Larsan/Ballmeyer, was manipulating the letter and blackmail plot.

witnesses.name: Epinay-sur-Orge railway employee
witnesses.statement: Identifies Robert Darzac as having arrived at Epinay-sur-Orge by train at about half-past ten on the night of the later attack, creating a damaging gap in Darzac’s alibi. The observation is true but is part of Larsan’s trap, since Darzac was lured away and delayed by Larsan’s accomplice.

witnesses.name: The anonymous writer of the Matin report
witnesses.statement: Publishes the first detailed public account of the Yellow Room case, including Daddy Jacques’s interview, the magistrate’s comments, the locked-room facts, the Bête du Bon Dieu, Mother Angenoux, and the expectation of Larsan’s return. The report preserves important early facts but is sensational and incomplete.

witnesses.name: Manager of the Epoque
witnesses.statement: Receives Rouletabille’s sealed letter naming the murderer before trial and is prepared to produce it if Rouletabille does not return. His role confirms that Rouletabille had reached the solution before leaving for America to gather proof.

witnesses.name: The builder of the pavilion
witnesses.statement: Consulted or expected to inspect the pavilion’s construction for traps, secret doors, passages, openings, ceiling cracks, or underground exits. The lack of such a route supports the final conclusion that the apparent locked-room attack did not occur while the room was locked.
